authorship_evidence: Project created pre-LLM era with consistent single-author commit history; standard intentionally-vulnerable teaching application

evidence.description: Arbitrary Python code execution via eval(). User input from request.form['expression'] (line 116) is passed directly to eval() on line 120 with no sanitization: str(eval(expression)). An attacker can execute arbitrary Python code such as __import__('os').system('id').

The result of eval(expression) is concatenated directly into an HTML response string without any output encoding or escaping. Even if eval() only returns data (not code execution), any HTML/JS in the result is rendered in the browser. The response is constructed via string concatenation: '"Result: " + (str(eval(expression))...'.

OS command injection via unsanitized user input. The address parameter from request.form['address'] (line 136) is concatenated directly into a shell command: rp('nslookup ' + address). The rp function references os.popen (imported on line 4). An attacker can inject arbitrary commands using shell metacharacters, e.g. 'example.com; cat /etc/passwd'.

The output of the OS command (nslookup result) is concatenated directly into the HTML response without escaping. The user-supplied address is also reflected in the output. An attacker can inject HTML/JS via the address parameter, which gets rendered in the browser unescaped.

XML External Entity (XXE) injection. The XML parser is created with etree.XMLParser(no_network=False, dtd_validation=True) on line 157, explicitly enabling network access and DTD validation. User-supplied XML from request.form['xml'] is parsed with etree.fromstring() on line 159 without disabling entity resolution (resolve_entities defaults to True in lxml). An attacker can read local files via <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'> or perform SSRF.

Parsed XML content is rendered directly into the HTML response without escaping on line 169: '(parsed_xml) if parsed_xml else ...'. An attacker can craft XML that, when parsed and rendered, includes HTML/JavaScript that executes in the victim's browser.

Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) via Jinja2. User input from request.form['name'] (line 189) is embedded into a template string using Python string formatting (%s) on line 204, then the template is rendered with render_template_string() on line 205. Since user input becomes part of the Jinja2 template before rendering, an attacker can inject template expressions like {{config}} or {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}} to achieve remote code execution.

Reflected XSS in the /sayhi endpoint. User input from request.form['name'] is placed into an HTML string via Python string formatting on line 189: '
Hello %s!

' %(request.form['name']). This string is then embedded into a Jinja2 template via %s substitution (not Jinja2's {{ }} autoescaping), so HTML/JS in the name parameter is rendered unescaped in the browser.

Flask secret key is hardcoded in source code: app.secret_key = 'Harry Potter And The Deathly Hallows'. This key is used to sign session cookies. An attacker who knows this key can forge arbitrary session data (e.g., set logged_in=True) to bypass authentication, or decrypt any signed data.

Passwords are stored in plaintext without hashing. On line 62, the registration handler stores the password directly from the form: User(username=request.form['username'], password=request.form['password']). The User model (line 26) stores passwords as plain strings with no hashing (e.g., bcrypt, scrypt). If the database is compromised, all user passwords are immediately exposed.

Flask debug mode is enabled in production: app.debug = True. This exposes the Werkzeug interactive debugger, which allows arbitrary code execution on the server if the debugger PIN is known or brute-forced. It also exposes detailed stack traces, source code, and local variables to any user who triggers an error.

The /evaluate endpoint allows arbitrary code execution but has no authentication check. Unlike the home route which checks session['logged_in'], the evaluate, lookup, xml, and sayhi endpoints are accessible to unauthenticated users, allowing anyone to exploit the code injection vulnerability without logging in.
The /lookup endpoint allows OS command injection but has no authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can access this endpoint and execute arbitrary shell commands via the address parameter.
The /xml endpoint allows XXE attacks but has no authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can access this endpoint and exploit the XXE vulnerability to read local files or perform SSRF.

The /sayhi endpoint allows SSTI/RCE but has no authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can access this endpoint and exploit the template injection vulnerability to achieve remote code execution.

The login query uses SQLAlchemy ORM's filter_by() method: User.query.filter_by(username=name, password=passw).first(). Although user input (name, passw) flows directly into the query, SQLAlchemy's ORM automatically parameterizes filter_by() arguments, preventing SQL injection. This is NOT vulnerable.

The error template renders {{ error }} using Jinja2's double-curly-brace syntax, which auto-escapes HTML by default. The error object (Flask's 404 error) is rendered safely. This is NOT vulnerable to XSS because Jinja2 autoescaping prevents HTML injection.

The index template renders {{data}} using Jinja2's auto-escaped double-curly-brace syntax. Even if user-controlled data were passed to this template variable, Jinja2 would HTML-escape it before rendering. In practice, the 'data' variable is not even passed from any route handler, so this is doubly safe. This is NOT vulnerable.

The CONFIG dictionary contains a hardcoded 'app_name' string: CONFIG = {'app_name': 'Damn Vulnerable Flask Application'}. While this looks like it could be a configuration with sensitive values, it only contains the application display name. This is NOT a credential or secret — it is a non-sensitive display string used in the HTML title on line 93.
